title: Multiple Sorts
description: Sort by one column, then by another.

slides.title: The Tie-Breaker Problem 👔
slides.content:
# What happens during a tie?

If you run `ORDER BY last_name`, and ten people share the last name 'Smith', how does the DB organize those ten Smiths?

It throws them in randomly again!

slides.title: Layering Orders 🍰
slides.content:
## A comma-separated list of orders

You can actually sort by multiple columns to act as tie-breakers. Just separate them with a comma.

`ORDER BY last_name, first_name`

This says: Firstly, sort perfectly by last name. But if there are any ties... organize those specific tied records by their first name.

quizData.question: If I write `ORDER BY state, city`, what does the database do?

options.text: It randomly picks if it wants to sort by state or by city
It sorts perfectly by State first. If multiple records share the exact same State, it sorts those tied records by City.
It sorts by City first, then State

quizData.explanation: SQL evaluates comma-separated ORDER BY fields sequentially. The first column is the primary sort. The second column is only used when the first column ties.

slides.title: Mixed Directions 🔀
slides.content:
## ASC and DESC together

You can even mix directions! Imagine a scoreboard. You want to sort by highest `score` descending, but if two players tied in score, you want to show the one who played the most `recent_date` descending.

`ORDER BY score DESC, recent_date DESC`

quizData.question: We want to see our students ordered by class 'grade' (A-Z Ascending). If they have the exact same grade, we want the older 'age' at the top (Descending).

options.text: ORDER BY grade DESC, age ASC
ORDER BY grade ASC, age DESC
ORDER BY age DESC, grade ASC

quizData.explanation: You can explicitly declare ASC or DESC for each individual column you sort by.

slides.title: Try it Out!
slides.content: Select everything from `users`. Order them first by `country` (A-Z default), and then by `salary` (highest to lowest).
